rectangle_with_two_holes.property: Simple rectilinear geometry with two axis-aligned holes
rectangle_with_two_holes.challenge: Low vertex count makes every vertex critical; removing any point causes large angular distortion on the right-angle corners.

cushion_with_hexagonal_hole.property: Curved outer boundary with a regular polygonal hole
cushion_with_hexagonal_hole.challenge: The hexagon's flat edges create large triangular displacement pockets - the simplifier must avoid removing the vertices that define those flat sides.

blob_with_two_holes.property: Irregular/organic outer boundary with two holes
blob_with_two_holes.challenge: The irregular outer ring has many equally-cheap vertices; greedy removal order matters because choosing the wrong cheap vertex can block larger savings on the holes.

wavy_with_three_holes.property: High-frequency undulating outer ring with three holes
wavy_with_three_holes.challenge: Adjacent wave peaks have similar displacement costs, so the heap must correctly rank near-equal candidates while keeping all three hole rings intact.

lake_with_two_islands.property: Large outer polygon representing a lake shore, two inner rings as islands
lake_with_two_islands.challenge: Island rings (interior of the outer ring, exterior of themselves) require correct signed-area orientation tracking; incorrect orientation handling would invert the islands.

original_01.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_02.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_03.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_04.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_05.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_06.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_07.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_08.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_09.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

original_10.challenge: Unknown structure; tests robustness on arbitrary real-world input.

many_holes.short: Many holes - 6 circular holes in a 3x2 grid
many_holes.challenge: The algorithm must preserve all 6 holes without accidentally merging or collapsing any ring. Managing a shared priority queue across 7 rings with equal budget pressure tests hole-count scalability.

dense_outer.property: High vertex count on a single ring (r = 1000 + 80*sin(8t), 400 samples)
dense_outer.challenge: Many near-collinear points with small but non-zero displacement. The simplifier must pick the globally cheapest removals without greedily eliminating almost-free vertices that block better savings later. Stresses heap throughput.

narrow_corridor.short: Narrow gap - 3-unit clearance between hole and outer ring
narrow_corridor.property: Near-degenerate topology: hole almost touches outer boundary
narrow_corridor.challenge: The clearance (3 units) is smaller than a typical simplification step, so the algorithm must detect when a chord would intersect the other ring and halt early. Tests geometric constraint enforcement.

nested_rings.short: Near-degenerate nesting - inner r=400, outer r=500
nested_rings.property: Thin annulus: outer ring and hole are geometrically close at all angles
nested_rings.challenge: Simplifying either ring risks sweeping a simplified chord across the other ring. Requires careful per-triangle displacement accounting and intersection detection around the full circumference.

large_with_many_holes.short: Large + many holes - 100-vertex outer, 8 irregular 6-vertex holes
large_with_many_holes.property: Combination of high vertex count and many holes with irregular hole shapes
large_with_many_holes.challenge: 9 simultaneous rings with non-uniform per-vertex displacement costs. The simplifier must budget displacement across rings and handle irregular (non-convex) hole geometry with unequal removal costs.

displacement-sweep.title: Summary and Interpretation
displacement-sweep.text: This is the rubric's areal displacement versus target vertex count plot. All three sweeps show that displacement decreases as more vertices are retained, but the rate depends strongly on topology. dense_outer drops smoothly from about 1.906e6 at 3 vertices to about 9.352e3 at 50 vertices and reaches 0 by 350 vertices, so a smooth single ring is easy to approximate once enough points remain. large_with_many_holes starts much higher, about 1.229e6 at 27 vertices and still 5.493e4 at 40 vertices, because nine interacting rings force large chord sweeps near the topology floor. many_holes decreases more gently, from about 8.337e4 at 21 vertices to about 3.848e3 at 37 vertices, showing that multiple well-separated holes are manageable when the rings are not packed tightly.

runtime-size.title: Summary and Interpretation
runtime-size.text: Runtime increases overall with input size, and the fitted curves summarize that trend, but the spread between cases shows that vertex count is not the only driver. dense_outer has 400 vertices yet finishes in about 0.090 s, while nested_rings has only 120 vertices and takes about 1.547 s, and large_with_many_holes has 148 vertices and takes about 5.412 s. That gap comes from rejected removals and repeated intersection checks on nearby or numerous rings. The fitted power law, c*n^0.252, is therefore descriptive for this benchmark set rather than evidence of sub-linear asymptotic complexity; the implementation still behaves more like O(n log n) with geometry dominating the constant factor.

memory-size.title: Summary and Interpretation
memory-size.text: Peak memory also rises overall with input size, but most small and medium cases stay in a narrow band of about 6 to 17 MB. The custom cases are representative: many_holes uses 6.375 MB, dense_outer uses 5.875 MB, nested_rings uses 7.125 MB, and large_with_many_holes uses 6.559 MB. The large jump comes from the biggest provided input, original_09, which reaches about 228.672 MB. So the fitted exponent, about 0.217, should be read mainly as a compact description of the current measurements; the underlying heap and ring structures still scale roughly linearly in space.

discussion.heading: Target Counts and Feasibility Limits
discussion.text: A requested target is a goal, not a guarantee, because the simplifier must preserve a valid polygon at every step. The clearest fixed-target example is rectangle_with_two_holes, where the requested target is 7 but the actual output remains at 11 because the three-ring topology already imposes a hard lower bound near three active vertices per ring. narrow_corridor shows the same phenomenon in a different way: once the 3-unit clearance becomes the limiting constraint, intersection checks reject almost every remaining candidate. Recording both requested and actual output counts is therefore necessary to distinguish safe early termination from ordinary underperformance.

discussion.heading: What the Custom Cases Show
discussion.text: The custom datasets confirm that topology matters more than raw vertex count. dense_outer stresses heap throughput with 400 near-collinear vertices, yet it runs quickly because it is a single smooth ring with no cross-ring interference. many_holes reaches its target exactly, showing that multiple rings are manageable when there is generous separation between them. nested_rings and large_with_many_holes are much harder even at lower vertex counts because many candidate chords must be checked against nearby or irregular holes, and large_with_many_holes is the clearest example: only 148 input vertices, but about 5.412 s runtime. narrow_corridor shows the same phenomenon at the extreme small end, where geometry rather than size determines when simplification must stop.

discussion.heading: Overall Interpretation
discussion.text: Taken together, the results support the main design goal of the greedy min-displacement approach: it consistently preserves valid topology, passes all provided tests, and improves on the reference displacement in 14 of the 15 provided cases. The main limitation is not raw throughput on large single-ring inputs, but pathological geometry with thin gaps, dense nesting, or many interacting holes. In other words, the implementation scales reasonably with input size, but its hardest cases are defined by geometric constraints rather than by vertex count alone.
